name: Camino Francés and Castrillo Mota de Judíos Loop
name_original: Castrillo de Mota Judíos desde Itero de la Vega — circular por Prado Carnicero
description: This moderate 13.6-mile mountain bike loop combines sections of the Camino Francés with local trails around Castrillo Mota de Judíos.
region_intro: Mountain biking around Itero De La Vega is characterized by the expansive, open plains of the Castilian plateau, offering long-distance cycling experiences. The region is a key part of the historic Camino Francés, providing routes that traverse agricultural landscapes and connect traditional villages. While the immediate area features mostly flat terrain ideal for gravel biking and touring, the nearby Canal de Castilla offers additional paths for less strenuous mountain biking. Best mountain bike trails around Itero De La Vega The most popular mountain bike trail is French Way: Villalcázar de Sirga Loop, a 17.9 miles (28.8 km) moderate trail that takes 2 hours 8 minutes to complete, following sections of the historic pilgrimage route. Another top favourite among local mountain bikers is Camino Francés and Castrillo Mota de Judíos Loop, a moderate 13.6 miles (21.9 km) path that explores the Camino Francés and passes through historic villages. Local mountain bikers also love the Canal of Castilla and Camino de Santiago — circular route by The King's Bridge from Frómista, a 43.1 miles (69.4 km) trail leading through the historic Canal de Castilla and Camino de Santiago, often completed in about 5 hours 2 minutes. Mountain biking around Itero De La Vega is defined by open plains, historic pilgrimage routes, and river paths, with routes offering options for different ability levels, from long-distance touring to more challenging sections. The routes in Itero De La Vega are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from 2 reviews. More than 20 mountain bikers have used komoot to explore Itero De La Vega's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many mountain bike trails are available around Itero De La Vega?
A: There are around 10 mountain bike trails in the Itero De La Vega area. These routes offer a mix of moderate and difficult options, with 9 routes rated as moderate and 1 as difficult, catering to various skill levels.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect when mountain biking near Itero De La Vega?
A: The terrain around Itero De La Vega is primarily characterized by the expansive, open plains of the Castilian plateau. You'll find mostly flat sections ideal for long-distance cycling, gravel biking, and touring. However, routes along the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/148691" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Canal de Castilla&lt;/a&gt; offer less strenuous paths, while the broader Montaña Palentina to the north provides more challenging, mountainous terrain for experienced riders.

Q: Are there any circular mountain bike routes in the area?
A: Yes, there are several circular routes. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1363804604/french-way-villalcazar-de-sirga-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;French Way: Villalcázar de Sirga Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 28.8 km circular trail, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1364630923/camino-frances-and-castrillo-mota-de-judios-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Camino Francés and Castrillo Mota de Judíos Loop&lt;/a&gt; offers a 21.9 km moderate option. For a longer, more challenging loop, consider the 69.4 km &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1156130085/canal-of-castilla-and-camino-de-santiago-circular-route-by-the-king-s-bridge-from-fromista" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Canal of Castilla and Camino de Santiago — circular route by The King's Bridge from Frómista&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What historical landmarks can I see along the mountain bike trails?
A: Many routes follow sections of the historic Camino de Santiago, offering glimpses of its rich heritage. You can encounter significant landmarks like the ancient Romanesque &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/849948" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente Fitero&lt;/a&gt;, which connects the provinces of Palencia and Burgos. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/148691" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Canal de Castilla&lt;/a&gt; itself is an 18th-century engineering marvel with features like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1012824" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Quadruple Lock&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there any family-friendly mountain bike trails near Itero De La Vega?
A: While there are no trails specifically marked as 'easy' in our current selection, the generally flat terrain around Itero De La Vega, particularly along the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/148691" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Canal de Castilla&lt;/a&gt;, can be suitable for families seeking less strenuous rides. These paths offer a peaceful environment away from technical challenges.

Q: What do other mountain bikers say about the trails in Itero De La Vega?
A: The mountain bike trails around Itero De La Vega are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Riders often praise the unique experience of cycling along the historic Camino Francés and the tranquil beauty of the Castilian plains.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the mountain bike trails?
A: Many of the trails around Itero De La Vega traverse open agricultural landscapes and rural paths, which are generally dog-friendly. However, it's always advisable to keep your dog under control, especially near livestock or in populated areas, and to check local regulations for specific sections of the Camino de Santiago or natural parks.

Q: Are there any places to explore or points of interest near the trails?
A: Beyond the trails, you can explore historical settlements like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6599008" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Castrojeriz&lt;/a&gt; or &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6599179" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Astudillo&lt;/a&gt;, which offer a glimpse into the region's past. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/148691" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Canal de Castilla&lt;/a&gt; itself is a significant point of interest, providing scenic routes and historical structures like its locks.

Q: What is the best time of year for mountain biking in Itero De La Vega?
A: The best time for mountain biking in Itero De La Vega is typically during spring (April-May) and autumn (September-October). During these seasons, the weather is generally milder and more pleasant for cycling, avoiding the intense heat of summer and the colder, potentially wetter conditions of winter.

Q: Are there any challenging mountain bike routes for experienced riders?
A: Yes, for experienced riders seeking a challenge, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1156130085/canal-of-castilla-and-camino-de-santiago-circular-route-by-the-king-s-bridge-from-fromista" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Canal of Castilla and Camino de Santiago — circular route by The King's Bridge from Frómista&lt;/a&gt; is rated as difficult, covering 69.4 km. For even more technical terrain, the Montaña Palentina region to the north offers higher elevations and more rugged trails.

Q: Where can I find parking for mountain biking in Itero De La Vega?
A: As Itero De La Vega is a small village on the Camino de Santiago, parking is generally available in or near the village center. For specific trailheads, it's often possible to find roadside parking or designated areas in nearby towns that serve as starting points for routes, especially those along the Camino or Canal de Castilla.

Q: Are there any mountain bike trails that pass through villages or offer amenities?
A: Many routes, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1363804604/french-way-villalcazar-de-sirga-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;French Way: Villalcázar de Sirga Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1364630923/camino-frances-and-castrillo-mota-de-judios-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Camino Francés and Castrillo Mota de Judíos Loop&lt;/a&gt;, traverse or pass near traditional villages. These villages often provide opportunities for refreshments, with local cafes, pubs, and restaurants where you can take a break and refuel.
tips: Itero is another small and pretty town on the Camino. Not only is it a pleasant little town but it also has cafes and hostels for the large number of people who come to make the pilgrimage.

The Itero Bridge, also called Paso Itero, Puente Fitero or Ponteroso, located over the Pisuerga River between Itero de la Vega and Itero del Castillo, is one of the longest bridges on the Camino de Santiago, with eleven ashlar arcades.
In the square there is a fountain although the water in summer is hot, and there is the center of Sephardic memory
You have to deviate a bit from the Camino de Santiago, next to the town that they changed a long time ago, from Matajudios to Motajudios
Here it is quite steep uphill and the ground is quite loose, but the descent is worth it :)

faqs.question: Where does the Camino Francés and Castrillo Mota de Judíos Loop start, and is there parking available?
faqs.answer: The loop officially starts and ends near Itero de la Vega. You can typically find parking within the village itself, which is a convenient base for beginning your ride.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this mountain biking loop?
faqs.answer: The terrain on this 21.9 km loop is varied, typical of the broader Camino Francés region. You can expect a mix of surfaces, including gravel paths, some stony tracks, and potentially sections of asphalt. While the overall difficulty is moderate, be prepared for some ascents and descents, especially around areas like the Prado Carnicero Ascent.

faqs.question: What can I see along the route?
faqs.answer: This loop offers a blend of historical and natural sights. You'll pass through the charming village of Itero de la Vega, cross the historic Puente Fitero, and explore the rich history at the Sephardic Memory Center and Motajudíos Archaeological Site. The Prado Carnicero Ascent also offers great views, including of Castrojeriz.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to mountain bike this loop?
faqs.answer: Spring (April-May) and Autumn (September-October) are generally the best times. The weather is usually milder, and the landscapes are vibrant. Summer can be very hot, especially in the Meseta region, and unexpected rain or sudden downpours can occur at any time of year, so always check the forecast.

faqs.question: Is the Camino Francés and Castrillo Mota de Judíos Loop dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the Camino Francés itself is generally dog-friendly, local regulations and specific trail conditions can vary. It's always best to keep your dog on a leash, especially when passing through villages or near livestock. Ensure you carry enough water for both yourself and your pet, as water sources might be limited on certain sections.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay any entrance fees for this trail?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to mountain bike this section of the Camino Francés or the local trails around Castrillo Mota de Judíos. It's a public route accessible to all.

faqs.question: Does this loop intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, this route incorporates a significant portion of the official Camino Francés, specifically the 'Camino Francés - 04 Burgos a León'. It also touches upon sections of 'Camiño Francés (Rutas alternativas)', 'R6 - Vega del Odra', and 'Camino a Palacios de Riopisuerga'.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this mountain biking loop?
faqs.answer: This 21.9 km loop has an estimated duration of approximately 1 hour and 50 minutes (6613 seconds) for mountain biking. This duration can vary based on your fitness level, the number of stops you make, and the weather conditions.

faqs.question: Are there any amenities like cafes or pubs along the route?
faqs.answer: As the loop passes through Itero de la Vega and the village of Castrillo Mota de Judíos, you will find some basic amenities. These villages, being part of the Camino Francés, often have small cafes, bars, or albergues where you can get refreshments or a meal.

faqs.question: Is this loop suitable for families with children?
faqs.answer: Given its moderate difficulty and varied terrain, this loop might be suitable for families with older children who have some mountain biking experience. Younger children or those new to mountain biking might find some sections challenging due to the distance and surface changes. Consider the fitness level and skill of all riders in your group.

faqs.question: What should I bring for a mountain bike ride on this loop?
faqs.answer: It's recommended to bring a well-maintained mountain bike, a helmet, sufficient water, snacks, a basic repair kit (spare tube, pump, tire levers), and appropriate clothing for the weather. Sunscreen and sunglasses are also advisable, especially during sunnier months, as parts of the route can be exposed.

waypoints.name: Prado Carnicero Ascent and View of Castrojeriz
